title: Database Design
summary: How a single PostgreSQL 15 database, organized schema-per-context with row-level org_id scoping, became the authoritative store for ordering, inventory, fulfillment, and settlement — and how a handful of carefully chosen constraints, not application code, are what actually enforce correctness under concurrency.

blocks.text: One PostgreSQL 15 database is the spine of KH Wholesale; every invariant that matters for money or stock is enforced at the database with constraints, not hoped for in application code.

paragraphs: I made an early and deliberate decision: KH runs on a single PostgreSQL 15 database, not a database-per-service sprawl. With a modular monolith and a 6-person team, one database bought me transactional integrity across bounded contexts — I can place an Order, write the per-supplier FulfillmentGroups, snapshot prices onto OrderLines, and append the OutboxEvent in one transaction that either fully commits or fully rolls back. That single property eliminated an entire class of distributed-consistency bugs we would otherwise have spent the next year chasing.

Inside that one database I used schema-per-context to keep the bounded contexts from bleeding into each other. Each module — identity, catalog, pricing, ordering, inventory, fulfillment, supplier, settlement, notifications — owns a Postgres schema. Cross-context reads go through well-defined module APIs, not ad-hoc joins across schemas, so the schema boundary doubles as an architectural fence. It is the same discipline a service boundary would give us, minus the network calls and the dual-write problem.

Tenant isolation is row-level on org_id. Every buyer or supplier is an Organization, and nearly every business table carries an org_id column. RBAC decides what a User can do; org_id scoping decides which rows they can even see. Application-layer query helpers inject the org_id predicate, and the columns are indexed so that scoping is also a performance lever, not just a security one.

The three design rules I refused to compromise on: money is always integer KRW stored as bigint, prices are snapshotted immutably at the moment they matter, and any invariant that protects money or stock is expressed as a database constraint. Everything below follows from those rules.

definitions.description: The Korean won has no minor units, so every amount is an integer number of won stored as bigint. No floats, no decimals, no currency-conversion ambiguity. All money math is integer; the only rounding in the system happens at fee calculation, governed by one documented rounding rule, so statements reconcile to the won.

When an Order is placed, the effective price (ContractPrice or PriceList) is copied onto each OrderLine as unit_price_krw and frozen. Later price-list edits never mutate historical orders. The OrderLine is the legal record of what was agreed, which is why it is treated as append-once and never updated in place for price fields.

One Postgres database, but each bounded context owns its own schema (ordering, inventory, fulfillment, settlement, ...). The schema namespace enforces module boundaries; cross-schema access is through module APIs rather than raw cross-schema joins.
Multi-tenant isolation is enforced per row via an org_id column referencing identity.organizations. Combined with RBAC, it guarantees a buyer org can never read or mutate another org's rows.

Instead of writing to Postgres and then publishing to SNS/SQS (a dual write that can half-fail), I write an OutboxEvent row in the same transaction as the state change. A relay polls the outbox and publishes to SNS, marking rows dispatched. The database commit is the single source of truth for 'did this event happen'.

blocks.caption: Core DDL for the ordering, inventory, and outbox tables. Money is bigint KRW; correctness invariants are CHECK and UNIQUE constraints, not application logic.
blocks.code:
-- Schemas mirror the bounded contexts.
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS ordering;
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS inventory;
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS messaging;

-- Explicit, table-driven enums for the state machines.
CREATE TYPE ordering.order_status AS ENUM (
  'DRAFT', 'PLACED', 'PARTIALLY_FULFILLED', 'PARTIALLY_CANCELLED',
  'COMPLETED', 'CANCELLED'
);
CREATE TYPE ordering.fulfillment_group_status AS ENUM (
  'AWAITING_SUPPLIER', 'ACCEPTED', 'FULFILLING', 'SHIPPED',
  'DELIVERED', 'SETTLED', 'REJECTED', 'CANCELLED', 'PARTIALLY_SHIPPED'
);

-- ORDER: buyer-level. Status is DERIVED from its FulfillmentGroups,
-- but persisted for fast reads. org_id scopes to the buyer org.
CREATE TABLE ordering.orders (
  id              bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  org_id          bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES identity.organizations (id),
  buyer_user_id   bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES identity.users (id),
  status          ordering.order_status NOT NULL DEFAULT 'DRAFT',
  currency        char(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'KRW' CHECK (currency = 'KRW'),
  subtotal_krw    bigint NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (subtotal_krw >= 0),
  tax_krw         bigint NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (tax_krw >= 0),
  total_krw       bigint NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (total_krw >= 0),
  placed_at       timestamptz,
  created_at      timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at      timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

-- FULFILLMENT GROUP: the per-supplier sub-order. The unit suppliers act on,
-- and the spine of ordering -> fulfillment -> settlement.
CREATE TABLE ordering.fulfillment_groups (
  id              bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  order_id        bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES ordering.orders (id),
  org_id          bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES identity.organizations (id), -- buyer org
  supplier_org_id bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES identity.organizations (id),
  status          ordering.fulfillment_group_status NOT NULL
                    DEFAULT 'AWAITING_SUPPLIER',
  subtotal_krw    bigint NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (subtotal_krw >= 0),
  accepted_at     timestamptz,
  created_at      timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at      timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  -- One supplier appears at most once per order: the split is deterministic.
  CONSTRAINT uq_group_per_supplier UNIQUE (order_id, supplier_org_id)
);

-- ORDER LINE: belongs to a FulfillmentGroup. Price is SNAPSHOTTED here
-- at placement and never mutated. amount_krw is derived but stored
-- and constrained so it can never drift from qty * unit_price.
CREATE TABLE ordering.order_lines (
  id                   bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  fulfillment_group_id bigint NOT NULL
                         REFERENCES ordering.fulfillment_groups (id),
  sku_id               bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES catalog.skus (id),
  qty                  integer NOT NULL CHECK (qty > 0),
  -- Immutable snapshot of the agreed price at placement time.
  unit_price_krw       bigint NOT NULL CHECK (unit_price_krw >= 0),
  price_source         text NOT NULL CHECK (
                         price_source IN ('CONTRACT', 'PRICE_LIST', 'PROMOTION')),
  amount_krw           bigint NOT NULL
                         GENERATED ALWAYS AS (qty * unit_price_krw) STORED,
  created_at           timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  -- A SKU cannot appear twice within one group: merge at cart time.
  CONSTRAINT uq_line_per_sku UNIQUE (fulfillment_group_id, sku_id)
);

-- INVENTORY ITEM: stock per Sku per location. The database is authoritative.
-- available can never go negative; the CHECK is the last line of defense
-- against oversell.
CREATE TABLE inventory.inventory_items (
  id           bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  sku_id       bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES catalog.skus (id),
  location_id  bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES fulfillment.locations (id),
  on_hand      integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (on_hand >= 0),
  reserved     integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (reserved >= 0),
  available    integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (available >= 0),
  -- Optimistic concurrency: bumped on every confirm/decrement.
  version      bigint  NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  updated_at   timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  CONSTRAINT uq_inventory_sku_location UNIQUE (sku_id, location_id),
  -- The accounting identity must always hold.
  CONSTRAINT ck_inventory_balance CHECK (available = on_hand - reserved)
);

-- STOCK RESERVATION: the confirmed (hard) hold. The Redis soft-hold has a TTL;
-- on supplier ACCEPT we confirm it into this row under a row lock.
CREATE TYPE inventory.reservation_status AS ENUM ('CONFIRMED', 'RELEASED', 'CONSUMED');
CREATE TABLE inventory.stock_reservations (
  id                   bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  inventory_item_id    bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES inventory.inventory_items (id),
  fulfillment_group_id bigint NOT NULL
                         REFERENCES ordering.fulfillment_groups (id),
  order_line_id        bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES ordering.order_lines (id),
  qty                  integer NOT NULL CHECK (qty > 0),
  status               inventory.reservation_status NOT NULL DEFAULT 'CONFIRMED',
  expires_at           timestamptz,  -- swept if a confirm never completes
  created_at           timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  -- At most ONE active reservation per line: the anti-oversell / anti-double-
  -- reserve guard. Partial index so released/consumed rows don't collide.
  CONSTRAINT uq_active_reservation_per_line
    EXCLUDE (order_line_id WITH =) WHERE (status = 'CONFIRMED')
);

-- OUTBOX: written in the SAME transaction as the state change it describes.
-- A relay polls undispatched rows and publishes to SNS, then marks them.
CREATE TABLE messaging.outbox_events (
  id             bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  aggregate_type text   NOT NULL,          -- e.g. 'Order', 'Shipment'
  aggregate_id   bigint NOT NULL,
  event_type     text   NOT NULL,          -- e.g. 'OrderPlaced'
  -- Idempotency anchor for consumers: (event_type, external_id) is unique.
  external_id    text   NOT NULL,
  payload        jsonb  NOT NULL,
  occurred_at    timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  dispatched_at  timestamptz,             -- NULL until the relay publishes
  CONSTRAINT uq_outbox_event_identity UNIQUE (event_type, external_id)
);
-- Hot path for the relay: only ever scans the small undispatched set.
CREATE INDEX ix_outbox_undispatched
  ON messaging.outbox_events (occurred_at)
  WHERE dispatched_at IS NULL;

paragraphs: A few of these choices are load-bearing and worth calling out. amount_krw on order_lines is a STORED generated column — the database computes qty * unit_price_krw so the line total can never drift from its inputs, even if buggy application code tries to set it directly. unit_price_krw is a frozen snapshot; nothing in the system updates it after placement, which is what makes an OrderLine a trustworthy legal record.

On inventory, three constraints work together. CHECK (available >= 0) is the hard floor that makes oversell impossible at the storage layer. CHECK (available = on_hand - reserved) keeps the three counters internally consistent so no code path can quietly desynchronize them. And the EXCLUDE constraint with a WHERE (status = 'CONFIRMED') predicate guarantees at most one active reservation per order line — a check-then-write race can no longer create a second hold. During the confirm transaction the application also takes SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the inventory_items row and acquires locks in sku_id order to avoid the deadlocks we hit early on.

On the outbox, uq_outbox_event_identity (event_type, external_id) gives every consumer a stable idempotency key, and the partial index ix_outbox_undispatched keeps the relay's poll cheap regardless of how many millions of dispatched rows have accumulated. The settlement ledger applies the same idea downstream with a UNIQUE on LedgerEntry per (group, type), which is what stopped the double-settlement incident when a carrier webhook retried ShipmentDelivered.

blocks.caption: Key indexes and the access pattern each one serves.
rows: Buyer order-history and ops dashboards, already org-scoped; supports the storefront p95 < 300ms target.
Supplier portal worklist: 'show me everything AWAITING_SUPPLIER for my org' without a full scan.
Enforces the deterministic per-supplier split; one group per supplier per order.
Forces cart-time merge; prevents duplicate SKU lines that would break settlement math.
One stock row per SKU per DC; the row that confirm locks with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE.
Anti-oversell guard: at most one active hold per line.
Drives the expiry sweeper that releases abandoned holds.
Consumer idempotency key; dedups SNS/SQS redeliveries.
Cheap relay polling over the small undispatched set only.

blocks.title: The constraints are the correctness layer
blocks.body: The invariants that actually keep KH solvent live in the schema, not in TypeScript. CHECK (available >= 0) makes overselling a SKU physically impossible at commit. CHECK (available = on_hand - reserved) stops the stock counters from drifting apart. EXCLUDE (order_line_id) WHERE status='CONFIRMED' permits at most one active hold per line, neutralizing the check-then-write race that caused ~12 oversell incidents/month before — now zero. The generated amount_krw column means a line total can never disagree with qty x unit_price_krw. uq_outbox_event_identity and the ledger's UNIQUE per (group, type) make event handlers idempotent by construction, which is how double settlement went away. Application code can have bugs; these rows still cannot enter an incorrect state. That is the whole bet: push every money- or stock-protecting invariant down to where the database can reject the bad write, and treat the service layer as orchestration on top of a store that refuses to lie.

metrics.label: Oversell incidents
metrics.note: available >= 0 CHECK + single-active-reservation EXCLUDE + FOR UPDATE confirm.

metrics.label: Order-placement error rate
metrics.note: One-transaction place: orders + groups + lines + outbox commit atomically.

metrics.label: Checkout p95
metrics.note: org-scoped indexes and re-pricing from Postgres rather than search.

metrics.label: Settlement disputes
metrics.note: integer KRW math, immutable OrderLine snapshots, append-only ledger.
